sentences.text: My name is Bent Lerno, B-E-N-T L-E-R-N-O.
My name at birth was Sally Levitin, L-E-V-I-T-I-N.
I got the name Shlomo, S-L-O-M-O.
January 22, 1927.
I am 69 years old.
I was born in a place called Charlottenlund, Copenhagen, Denmark.
I was, like I said, lived in a suburb called Charlottenlund.
We lived in a, I guess, a fairly nice home.
But my father wanted to build a basement under it.
In those days, also, there was no basement.
And the house started falling down into the basement.
So the engineers came out and said that, unfortunately, they couldn't do anything.
And the house should be torn down.
So my father instead built the first high rise outside Copenhagen, which was a five-story apartment house.
And it was finished in 1928.
And it is still standing there today.
I had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three brothers and a sister.
My oldest brother-- I had two that had passed away before I was born.
So I thought that there was no sense of naming them.
But my oldest brother, Richard, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was born in 1910 and-- no, I'm sorry. 1911.
This year he would be 85 years old.
My second brother which passed on was Harry.
And I have a sister, Ida, I-D-A, Ida.
Her married name was Goldine.
And she was married to a  doctor, which later became my doctor until he passed away.
And then my brother Paul, which is about two years older than I am.
Paul and I have been, I would say, real good friends all those years and have enjoyed each other's company.
Like, I was just in Denmark visiting him.
But we'll come to that later.
We lived in Charlottenlund till about 1930.
Then my father bought a villa-- what we called in Denmark a villa-- in a town called Hellerup.
It was a little closer to Copenhagen.
But it was, like, a private home.
Before that, we lived in our own apartment house up on the fifth floor.
But it was too much for my mother to run up and down five flights of stairs.
And in those days, elevators wasn't modern.
So that is one of the reasons.
And so we moved to Hellerup in 1930.
And I was put into a kindergarten, which I walked to every day myself until I started school.
And I started school in 1933.
And that was Hellerup School, which just two years ago had a 100 years anniversary.
So it is still standing and still used as a school today, is used as an international school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's-- I went to, to school.
And I graduated at an early age because in Denmark, you don't have to-- if you don't have, what shall I say, the ambition to go the academic way, then-- I chose not to.
And I chose then-- in Denmark, it's known that you would become an apprentice.
And I was lucky to get an apprenticeship in a big mechanical company.
But it was a little more taught the old-fashioned way of whatever you made, you made your own things, like blacksmithing and, and things like this.
And it was, it was a hard journey, you know.
On bicycle it took an hour, on bicycle to-- every day you had to be there at 7 o'clock in the morning, that meaning that I had to leave the house before 6:00.
And I had to be up at 5:00, you know.
And so it was, it was hard time.
And in Denmark, it gets awfully cold in the wintertime.
And I think in 1942 and 1943 was the hardest winters that we ever had until actually this year, where it beats all records, you know.
It, it got so cold that you could actually walk on the ice between Denmark and Sweden.
And that's about eight miles across.
So of course, so our people shouldn't escape from Denmark, the Germans sent their ice breakers out all the time, you know, to clear the strait.
And-- excuse me-- that was actually where we were going to school, enjoying-- we had a summer home outside Copenhagen what was in a place called Hornbaek.
It was such a beautiful ride up to the summer house.
It was called the, the French Riviera in, in, in Denmark, you know.
You had to drive up along the coastline.
And it's all water.
And it's, it's really beautiful to, to see.
And so we had a fairly good time.
And when it was a big Jewish holidays, we always went to, to synagogue.
And we had family, friends, like the Ginsberg, the [NON-ENGLISH], and whatever else people or some friends.
And those were people I knew because my parents played bridge with the elder group.
But on  big holidays, we always got together.
And of course, around holiday time, we-- my mother had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two, three sisters.
The one we didn't see so often.
She lived far away.
But the other two lived right around us in, in Hellerup and Charlottenlund.
So on one holiday, we were at our house.
And on the next holiday, or the second holiday, we were at my one aunt's house, and so on and so forth.
So the family were always together.
And that first start breaking up when everybody, my older brothers and my sister got married.
And, and my aunt and uncle's children also got married.
And it kind of fizzled out, like one say, because then you get your own family unit.
And that was what it was.
But we lived.
We went to the synagogue in Copenhagen, and, and-- you have to excuse me-- and so on the big holidays because it was quite a ride to, to get in there.
And of course, we had-- it wasn't so much that you just came to the synagogue.
That was the part that you always looked at, you know, and so that you had to take the train [INAUDIBLE].
You could always buy the ticket the day before.
And therefore, you didn't use money.
But that was what you had to do.
And I remember the big holidays-- holiday was standing from early morning till late night, you know, in the synagogue.
And then we went home.
And then my father asked us to come back.
We cried.
And we said, no, it takes so long.
And it was very hard, you know.
But at that time, we had a rabbi.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH].
He was of German extraction, you know, and asked to come to Denmark to become a rabbi.
But he had a funny thing that he really didn't try to learn Danish.
And so he was very hard to understand.
And I had my bar mitzvah with him, or confirmation-- whatever you said in those days.
And he was, he was almost impossible.
And I thought many times it would be nicer to go out skiing instead of going in there on Sunday and going to religious teaching, you know.
And so we never had this pressure, you could say, with knowing you were Jewish.
I knew only from in school.
I didn't attend the religious hour that they had.
So that day, I could go and play or do whatever I wanted.
And I thought that was neat, you know.
And so nobody ever really bothered us, you know.
And so you, you never thought about the difference.
And I can tell you in relation to this that when I came to the United States, there was so many that asked me, what are you?
I said, well, I'm Danish.
And they said, well, Danish like-- then what is the religion in Denmark?
Oh, I said, the main religion is Lutheran.
Oh, oh.
Then they turned around.
They they kind of asked, are you Lutheran?
I said, no, I'm Jewish.
Oh, that was what we asked in the first place.
I said, why?
Because I had never been asked in Denmark.
So I thought it was funny that I should asked here.
But of course, here things were changing.
But-- so everything was going real great in Denmark.
Like I say, I attended   my work.
And when I had my vacation, I went up to our summer place.
And I enjoyed all the company of friends and relatives and whatever you have.
And so I can only say at, at that point, we, we lived a pretty  good life until it was September, exactly the 30th of September 1943.
We had, of course, a new rabbi, Marcus Melchior, which was the assistant rabbi.

He was born in Denmark and raised and-- but he was still the assistant rabbi, so-- but people were wondering when he came in on September 30, 1943 and wasn't in his robe and his round kippah, which they used in those days.
And he came in.
And he said, everybody, please sit down and be quiet.
I have something to announce to you.
He said, I have gotten it on good authority.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He said, I can't name the man's name right now, but the prime minister of Denmark, or former prime minister of Denmark, Hans Hedtoft, have called me and said that some weeks prior, the Germans had broken into the Jewish office, religious office in Copenhagen of the rabbi and the whole congregation, you know.
And they took the list of the names of all the Jews in Denmark.
And we didn't know why, you know, they wanted the copies of everybody, you know.

And we never thought about it, and-- but now we have it on good authority at, on Yom Kippur, which is October 1, the Germans are coming and want to take all the Jews and put them in a concentration camp.

So he said, I beg of you to go home, right now, all of you that is here, and start calling your friends, your relatives, all you can, and have them again call others and et cetera, et cetera, so that it's spread out.
And I got, that afternoon, a telephone call.
And I was 16 years old.
And I said to my mother, something is really wrong.
Tomorrow, when it's Yom  Kippur, and we were going to go to the Ginsbergs', it's not going to be anything, because we all have to get out, because the Germans are going to take the Jews.
And of course, at first, my mother didn't believe it.
So she said-- I said, I am leaving, and my brother Paul is leaving, and we had a friend over.
And he said, I'm  going with you.

So we just packed a little bit in a little box and paper bags or whatever we could find, you know, to just enough-- put enough clothes on so we could be warm, because it was really cold already in October.
My mother said she would stay with friends and wait for my father to come back from the city.
And of course, we had Germans living right across the street from us.
It was German officers.
It was like they was-- they had this villa that was across where they lived.
And, and they could enjoy life when they were away from Germany itself.
And then we used the back yard.
We had cut a hole in the fence.
And it was family of the famous painter Gauguin.
They lived in the back of us.
So we slipped out there.
We went to the railroad station.
We had a little bit of money with us.
And then we took the train up to our summer town.
But we were, again, afraid of staying in our summer home, because we presumed that the Germans knew that, that was where we would be.
So we stayed with some very good friends.
There was a set of twins-- Philip and Theo-- Philip and George Mattsen.
And so we stayed with them.
And they said, nah, it's probably just a false alarm and whatever you have.
And don't worry about it.
We will stay here for a couple of days.
And we will see what happens.
Excuse me.
Did you ever hear what was going on in Germany and Eastern Europe at this time?
We knew that something was going on.
But of course, Germany had a very good propaganda machine.
And therefore, all what was, was known to be bad and so on was made in light.
This is just hearsay, and, and-- but we did see pictures, some pictures that had been smuggled out.
And so-- but you can't really describe what it-- how can I say it-- how it really was there until maybe you saw it, you know?
And so it wasn't really in our minds to know-- we knew that something was going on.
We knew that things wasn't in Denmark as they had been before because, of course, the Germans took everything there was to take.
They took butter, sugar.
And they had first right to everything, to meat and so on.
So for the Danes, everything got rationed, you know.
They took all the cars from dealerships and shipped them to Germany.
And they took all our food and everything else.
So we were going around with ration cards.
We couldn't get tobacco, couldn't get anything.
So it was pretty bad.
But anyway, we were up in the summer town.
And we called our friend's house in Copenhagen.
My mother said, I'm coming up today with your aunt and uncle and his brother.
And we will go to their little cabin.
And then we will contact you.
We know where you are.
He said, yeah, we are right across on Church Street, you know, from the church itself-- the family is called Mattsen, you know.
And my mother said, yeah, I know the boys well.
So I said fine.
My father hadn't finished business yet.
And he  was trying to get friends to come into our house to stay there so the Germans wouldn't see it open.
So they came there every day, even though they didn't live there, you know, and took care of the house.
And so now we are sitting up and waiting for my mother.
And then finally, she came up to the summer town.
Then it was my uncle's and uncle's brother, Uncle Leopold and his brother, [NON-ENGLISH], and my Aunt Olga.
They had a friend.
He was next door to their little cabin, you know.
And he said he would help them.
So my mother said, why don't we have him arrange things and so on?
And we said OK.
What else could we say?
So we were going to go now outside of the city toward the south of the city of Hornbaek, which is just a summer town of about 3,000 people.
So we went through the forest.
And we know the Germans were lying in the forest, and, and they were protecting the sea coast, they say, of Denmark.
So they had built the concrete internment there and had big cannons sitting there and everything else.
But you couldn't see it from the road, because it was behind the forest.
So anyway, we went to this fisherman's house.
It was about 4 kilometers, which is about 3 miles south of Hornbaek.
It was a little red house on the side.
And it had, like, a little outhouse which they stored furniture, wooden furniture, and so on and so forth.
And we had to be there at 8 o'clock.
And so my brother Paul, my friend, Ralph, and myself, we took bicycles.
My mother and her family took the bus, and they--
My aunt and uncle, his name was Leopold Abrahamson.
He originally came down to Denmark from Norway.
So he has S-O-N in the end of his name instead of S-E-N.
And my uncle's brother, [NON-ENGLISH], was also an Abrahamson.
And my Aunt Olga, of course, born Solamon like my mom.
And those are the ones, they had no children.
So they came as they were.
So now we go to this house.
And we're sitting in the outhouse.
And we are waiting, and we are waiting, and we are waiting, and no fishing boat, no-- nothing is happening.
And you're sitting there.
And it's cold.
And it's rainy.
And finally, around 11 o'clock, somebody comes in very quietly, knock on the door and say, you better go home, because we was just told that there is no fishing boat coming tonight.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So we had figured on leaving the bicycles there.
And they were going to be picked up the next day.
So we said, well, us three will take the bicycles.
And my mother, again, with her family, took the bus that's going in the other direction.
And it's coming-- the last bus is coming from the city of Helsingor, going  up towards Hornbaek.
And that would be there about-- something like 10 past 11:00.
So we waited until we saw they were aboard.
And the bus took off.
And so us three on bicycles, we followed the bus into the forest.
Now, when we come into the forest ourself, when we came closer up toward the city of Hornbaek, we see the bright lights on the brakes of this bus.
And once we saw it stopped, we knew something was wrong, because the bus never stopped there.
And what it was-- and we had been sold out by my uncle's friend next door.
We didn't know that he was a Nazi sympathizer, even though they had been friends with him for 25 years.
And for $5, I think, something like $5 apiece, we were sold out.
And how we found out afterwards was that my friend went to the bus driver when we saw the bus had come into the city of Hornbaek and nobody was there.
And he said that the Germans had boarded the bus.
And they stood with a list with seven names on and read it off.
And they took my mother.
And all-- they took the four people that were on the bus.
But they knew specifically who they were.
So when they asked for the names, and they said, yes, that's me and, yes, it's me.
And of course, they said, please, step out.
And they had to step out of the bus.
And that we had seen the stop, we figured something was, like I said, something was wrong.
So we said, let's get the hell out of here.

And I think that with the speed of bicycles to [INAUDIBLE] forest and  upper back road, and around the whole back way, it's like another 6 miles around till you can go a back road and then come into the city on the back side and then to the friend's house.
I would say at that time, I would have set the speed record of bicycling in a dark forest, because we were that scared.
We had no lights.
And, and the rain was coming down slowly, but still it was cold.
And so we finally come into the city of Hornbaek.
And we said, what is happening, you know?
And so our friend said, we will sneak from here, walk, because we had curfews, you know.
And after 10 o'clock, we couldn't go out, you know, and-- but they snuck out anyway.
And like I say, they went to the chauffeur of the bus.
And he said he don't know what happened.
But he said they had their names.
They had everything.
And first, we didn't know who to blame.
But we said, the only ones we have talked to is you guys.
And the only one is the restaurateur that owns a big restaurant, the Farmhouse, in Hornbaek.
And then also I think that was the only one that my mother had talked to.
And so, of course, later on there was others that he gave up.
And there was a special-- someone from the underground that he sold out to the Germans, which was taken.
And then they knew that it could only be them, because they had been at the restaurant that night before.
And the next day, they were taken by the Germans.
And they knew that he owned the restaurant.
So that would be the only one it could be.
So by the underground court, he was condemned and later was executed.
I can't remember his name.
I-- that I have to find out sooner or later, you know.
And I never thought about getting his name who owned it.
But you see, people have passed on.
And many of my friends, you know, have gone, and-- so there's very few that even that you can ask anymore where is this and who was that, and so on and so forth.
But I never thought about it.
But next time I go to Denmark I definitely would try and get his name, you know, because it was also not an important person in the whole scenario, but he was one that really made it miserable.
So we tried a couple of other times to, to get across.
And it was canceled before.
They said the water was too choppy.
And there was too many patrol boats out, and so on.
But on the 6th-- actually we started on the 5th of October-- we were told there was a place called Dronningmolle.
It's like the Queen's Mill.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a little city also of maybe some 100 people, you know.

And, and they had a big cement factory and roof tile-- those red roof tile-- factory that was lying off the road and then down onto the beach, because that's where they stored all the big pipes and cement blocks and everything else what you have.
We were told to meet there at 8 o'clock.
And so our friends took us there, and--
That was myself, my brother Paul, my friend Ralph Ginsberg, then my father.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then there was other people.
There was a famous Danish musician called [NON-ENGLISH].
